Ginia Bellafante reviews various fashions shown during fall 2001 collections that ended in Paris last week; photo (M)

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. said slow demand from networking companies might drag down the pace of recovery — expected to be driven by new orders from personal computer makers — in the second half.. "Networking is still a litt

THE MARKETS: STOCKS & BONDS; Shares Decline as Outlook for Technology Stirs Concern
Nasdaq composite index falls 44.32 points, to 2,170.78; Dow Jones industrial average drops 54.91 points, to 10,922.09; Standard & Poor's 500-stock index declines 10.57 points, to 1,254.39; graph; chart (M)
Advertising; The upfront market for TV time gets curiouser and curiouser with CBS's claims on performance.

THE RATE CUT: THE IMPACT; Just in Time, Or Too Late?
Analysis: economists feel Federal Reserve's cuts in interest rates will not prevent recession; say cuts are not enough to end thousands of layoffs companies are making, nor will cuts persuade businesses to buy more equipment when they already own too much; say stocks may rise after cuts, but lower borrowing costs have always needed months, even year, to affect people's decisions about most big purchases; say because of this delay, economy will continue to become weaker before it can recover; photos (M)

PORTFOLIOS, ETC.; It May Still Be Too Soon to Plunge Back Into Argentina
Jonathan Fuerbringer column urges caution on investing in Argentina in view of financial crisis that seems far from over; graph (M)

THE MARKETS: STOCKS & BONDS; Weak Economic Data Take a Toll on Nasdaq and Dow
Weak Economic Data Take a Toll on Nasdaq and Dow**Dow Jones industrial average falls 80.03 points, or 0.7 percent, to 10,796.65; Nasdaq composite index drops 74.4 points, or 3.4 percent, to 2,146.20; Standard & Poor's 500-stock index drops 18.85 points, or 1.5 percent, to 1,248.58; yield on 10-year Treasury note falls to 5.20 percent from 5.28 percent; price rises 20/32, to 98 16/32; 30-year bond rises 30/32, to 96 9/32; yield falls to 5.63 percent from 5.70 percent; graphs; charts (M)

HORSE RACING; Scientists Try to Find Answer for Dead Foals
Scientists and veterinarians in Kentucky work to pinpoint why hundreds of pregnant mares have lost their foals in past two weeks; they report rate of stillborn foals has slowed and reiterate theory that deaths may have been caused by fungus in bluegrass, but they still have nothing conclusive (M)

Economy Remained Weak in May As Employers Continued Job Cuts
BLS reports economy remained weak in May as employers continue to cut jobs, although at slower pace than during previous two months; unemployment rate falls to 4.4 percent from 4.5 percent, mostly by decline in number of people looking for jobs; college-educated white-collar workers are among first to lose jobs in this downturn; manufacturing jobs and orders fall, although construction employment increases ahead of last year's pace, partly because lower interest rates encourage people to buy or remodel homes; graphs (M)

For Mets' Zeile, Power Shortage Raises Concern
MIAMI, June 2 — The way Todd Zeile sees it, after 10 major league seasons, he can be expected to average around .268, hit 20 home runs and drive in 80 runs.

A Significant Lift for a Long-Ailing U.S. Industry
Steel industry executives hail as long overdue Bush administration's bid to limit steel imports; analysts say move could provide relief to industry suffering from wave of bankruptcies and minimal profitability (M)

As Storm Moves On, 2 States Tally Damage
South Texas and Louisiana begin drying out from Tropical Storm Allison, which caused flooding and widespread devastation and left 22 people dead in Houston area; photo (M)

Bond Sale Seems to Signal Easing of Argentine Crisis
Argentine government succeeds in auctioning $700 million in treasury bills at far lower interest rates than it has been forced to pay in recent months; this signals that country's financial crisis has begun to ease; sale, at annual yields of 7.89 percent for three-month notes and 9.90 percent for six-month notes, is in line with, if not slightly better than, economists' expectations (M)

THE MARKETS: Market Place; Future May Be More Uncertain For Technology
Market Place column on Merrill Lynch report showing technology shares remain expensive, even at their current depressed levels; report holds true earnings of such companies began to deteriorate three years ago, before economy soured, even as their reported earnings soared; cites use of stock options in lieu of higher salaries as big contributor to reported earnings (M)

Results Might Not Be Known for Days : Violence Goes On As Filipinos Count
In the race for control of the Philippine Congress, claims of victory continued to pour in Tuesday as the country awaited the outcome of the counting of ballots, which may go on for days..All that appeared certain was that more lives would be lost in s

Corporate Profits in Big Drop As Personal Spending Rises
Commerce Department lowers estimate of GNP growth in second quarter to annual rate of 1.2 percent from 1.3 percent; graph (S)

THE MARKETS: STOCKS & BONDS; Technology Continues Losses as Investors Take Profits
Nasdaq composite index closes down 55.67 points, to 2,029.12; Dow Jones industrial average falls 66.94 points, to 10,472.12, while Standard & Poor's 500-stock index declines 13.23 points, to 1,202.45; graph; chart (M)

Chrysler Loss Isn't as Deep As Expected
DaimlerChrysler says its Chrysler unit lost $125 million in second quarter, better performance than analysts had expected because of stronger auto sales and gains from turnaround plan; Chrysler earned $986 million in year-earlier quarter; it lost $1.2 billion in first quarter; second-quarter net income at DaimlerChrysler fell 58 percent, to about $681 million; second-quarter earnings fell to $453 million from $1.52 billion; revenue dropped to $35.1 billion from $38 billion; DaimlerChrysler chief executive Jurgen Schrempp says company is on track to post operating profit for year of $1 billion to $1.5 billion; Chrysler chief executive Dieter Zetsche comments (M)

Readers' Comments From Eyewitnesses
New York Times on the Web readers respond to the attacks on the World Trade Center and the Pentagon. To send your own comments, send e-mail to shareyourthoughts@nytimes.com .

Text of Federal Reserve Statement
The following is a press release issued by the Federal Reserve on April 18, 2001.

In Surprise Move, Fed Cuts Interest Rates by Half Point
WASHINGTON, April 18 - Faced with growing signs that businesses are retrenching rapidly as the economy weakens, the Federal Reserve cut its benchmark interest rate by half a percentage point today, igniting a powerful rally on Wall Street.

International Paper Posts $44 Million Loss
International Paper Co says it lost $44 million in first quarter; sales are $6.9 million, up from $6.4 million in year-earlier period (S)

THE RATE CUT: THE OVERVIEW; FED, IN A SURPRISE, CUTS RATES AGAIN, SPURRING MARKETS
Federal Reserve cuts its benchmark interest rate by half percentage point, igniting powerful rally on Wall Street; federal funds target rate on overnight loans between banks is lowered to 4.5 percent from 5 percent, and discount rate on loans to banks from Federal Reserve system goes to 4 percent from 4.5 percent; this is fourth half-point cut in rates by central bank since beginning of year, and Fed signals there may be more cuts in coming months; decision reflects Fed's increasing concern that economy is at risk from steep falloff in corporate profits and related pullback by companies in purchase of equipment; Fed also cites concern that drop in many stock prices would lead consumers to rein in spending; move surprises most investors, but Alan Greenspan, Fed chairman, sometimes acts when market's guard is down to give his moves maximum impact; stock prices shoot up immediately, with Dow Jones industrial average gaining 399.10 points to close at 10,615.83 and Nasdaq composite index finishing at 2,079.44, up 156.22 points; photos; graphs (L)

Market Place: The Fed Gets a Case of the Nerves
The Federal Reserve is growing more worried about the economy. Investors figure that means everything will work out fine.

Fed's Statement on Action by Its Monetary Panel
Text of statement by Federal Reserve after Federal Open Market Committee, Fed's monetary policy panel, decides to lower interest rates

Fed, in a Surprise, Cuts Rates Again, Spurring Markets
WASHINGTON, April 18 — Faced with evidence that businesses are retrenching rapidly in a weakening economy, the Federal Reserve cut its benchmark interest rate by half a percentage point today, igniting a powerful rally on Wall Street.

THE MARKETS: Market Place; The Fed Gets a Case Of the Nerves
Market Place column on investors' enthusiastic reaction to Federal Reserve's decision to cut interest rates for fourth time this year in effort to check falling rates of business investment and corporate profit growth; graphs; photo (M)

MARKET WATCH; A Splash of Cold Water on Technology Stocks' Revival
Gretchen Morgenson column on view of Steven Milunovich of Merrill Lynch that both investors and analysts have overly optimistic expectations for recovery of technology companies, their profits and their share prices; graph (M)

Economists Say Recession Is Unlikely
Survey by National Assn for Business Economics finds business economists still believe US economy will probably avoid recession in 2001 even though chances for recession have increased (S)

THE MARKETS: Market Place; Bond Market Gives Signals Of an Upturn
Recent performance of US government bonds suggests that these investors are already looking ahead to economic rebound; over last two months, longer-term Treasury securities have been selling off, pushing their yields higher, while shorter-term rates have been relatively stable (M)

Another Cut by the Fed
Editorial lauds Federal Reserve decision, cutting federal funds rate by 0.5% for fifth time since beginning of year and reaffirming strong commitment to averting potential recession; says challenge facing Fed is to bolster consumer spending, which accounts for two-thirds of econoimic activity, thereby staving of recession until businesses can once again ramp up capital investments (M)

CITING DOWNTURN, FED LOWERS RATES 5TH TIME THIS YEAR
Federal Reserve again cuts benchmark federal funds rate, from 4.5 percent to 4 percent, to spur economy; cites deteriorating business outlook; cut is 5th since Jan, when rate was 6.5 percent; banks respond by cutting prime lending rates to 7 percent, from 7.5 percent (M)

Text of Fed's Statement
Text of statement by Federal Reserve after Federal Open Market Committee decides to lower interest rates (M)

Central Bank Warns Of Future Weakness After Half-Point Move : Fed Trims Rates and Hints at More Cuts
The American central bank reduced short-term interest rates by half a percentage point on Tuesday, the fifth easing this year, and warned the economy remained under pressure, raising speculation of another cut late in June.. The Federal Open Market Commi

Mutual Funds Heavily Sold Cisco Shares in First Quarter
Three of largest mutual fund companies--FMR, parent of Fidelity funds; Janus Capital; and Putnam Investments--led exodus from Cisco Systems in first quarter, selling more than 250 million shares, or 3.5 percent of its outstanding shares; sales were factor in Cisco's decline to low of $13.63 in early April from $42.63 in late January; chart (M)

Investing; Why Funds See Daimler as a Trade-In
Mutual fund managers and observers comment on why funds have been jettisoning shares of DaimlerChrysler AG since acquisition was announced in 1998; one contributing factor is that Chrysler is not longer in S&P 500 index; table (M)

MARKET WATCH; A Company Tested And Found Wanting
Gretchen Morgenson Market Watch column says Nortel Networks' second-quarter loss of $19.2 billion represents 'milestone in the history of mismanagement of shareholder assets'; says $12.3 billion writedown in good will on recent acquisitions is particularly staggering; graph (M)

43% Decline in Morgan Stanley Earnings
Morgan Stanley Dean Witter & Co earned $705 million in fiscal third quarter ended Aug 31, down from $1.25 billion year earlier; revenue fell 16 percent, to $5.27 billion from $6.31 billion; firm, largest single tenant in World Trade Center, says its short-term business outlook turned 'decisively negative' after terrorist attacks (M)

A NATION CHALLENGED: THE ECONOMY; CONGRESS GETS PLEA TO DROP TAX BENEFITS FOR INVESTORS AND WIDEN ECONOMIC RELIEF
Federal Reserve chairman Alan Greenspan and former treasury secretary Robert Rubin advise Congress against Republican proposals to cut capital gains tax or corporate income taxes to stimulate lagging economy that has been further weakened by effects of Sept 11 terrorist attack; Rubin, in third appearance on Capitol Hill since attack, joins Greenspan in warning that approach would not provide immediate punch for rebound; photo of two with Sen Max Baucus at Senate Finance Committee hearing; Conference Board reports consumer confidence in sharp decline since attack; other data show steep falloff in retail sales and at least temporary job losses in New York; Greenspan and Rubin recommend only temporary tax cuts that would not create long-term budget problems or push up long-term interest rates at time when federal surplus projections are being scaled back even more (M)
NEWS SUMMARY

Booming New York Skirts Trend of Job Losses
New data from New York State Department of Labor find that labor market in New York City is booming; jobless rate in January fell sharply to 5.1 percent from 5.6 percent; other statistics discussed (M)

Stocks End Higher on Hopes of Economic Turnaround
Technology stocks rose today as investors were unfazed for now by new corporate profit warnings and a dour economic outlook.

Yahoo C.E.O. to Step Aside as Company Warns on Earnings
After holding the stock market in suspense for most of the day, Yahoo Inc. announced this afternoon that its chief executive, Timothy Koogle, would step aside and that its first-quarter financial results would miss Wall Street's already-reduced forecasts.

New Worries Of Slowdown Spur Canada To Cut Rates
Canadian central bank, worried that United States economy is losing steam more rapidly than expected and that its appetite for goods from Canada will flag, reduces short-term interest rates by half percentage ponts to try to stimulate domestic demand; graph (M)

In the Region/Westchester; Residential Sales Off to a Strong Start in 2001
Westchester County, NY, housing inventories are tight, so sellers often receive multiple bids; sales are down; prices are up; photo; graph (In the Region/Westchester) (M)

Japan Is Shackled by Deflation, Blocking Its Hope for Recovery
Deflation is major cause of many of Japan's economic woes, blocking any hope for recovery as country shows signs of falling back into recession; rare but devastating drop in prices of goods and services is feeding on itself, chipping away at asset values, increasing credit risks, pinching wages and salaries, and preventing economy from generating any sustained growth after decade of stagnation; stocks trade at same prices of 15 years ago, and land values in major cities which back many loans made by Japanese banks have fallen 65 percent from peak in 1991; excluding fresh foods, prices nationwide fell 1.1 percent in 2000 in greatest decline since World War II; though some economists have championed deflation as road to economic modernization, question is whether Japan can tolerate its pain; charts; photo (M)

AS STOCKS TUMBLE, PRESIDENT SOUNDS A NOTE OF CONCERN
Pres Bush says he is 'concerned' that many Americans' stock portfolios are being affected by steep sell-off in first two months of his presidency; speaks only minutes after market opens with 300-point plunge in Dow Jones industrial average; he seems intent on conveying at least glancing sense of optimism after months in which Democrats have accused him of talking down the economy as way of building support for his $1.6 trillion tax cut; declines in Dow and in Nasdaq composite index make Bush and his advisers increasingly aware that his political standing is likely to be affected by his economic management skills over next few months; during presidential campaign he insisted that economic boom was product of entrepreneurship rather than policy decisions in Washington; now he argues that 'good public policy' is path to 'second wind for economic growth'; Bush justifies on economic grounds his decision to back off campaign pledge to regulate levels of carbon dioxide emitted by power plants; he suggests that such regulations would add to burden on consumers at time of economic downturn; photo (M)

Fading Yen Casts a Shadow On Japanese Leader's Visit
Pres Bush prepares to meet with Japan's evidently lame-duck prime minister, Yoshiro Mori, as White House debates whether to allow rapid devaluation of yen; move could ease Japan's growing financial crisis, but it is strongly opposed by American auto industry and others that compete with Japan, whose exports would become less expensive; choices facing Bush are neither easy nor pleasant; he is acutely aware that his father's political troubles in 1991 and 1992 were rooted partly in perception that he never got handle on dealing with Japanese competition at time of American economic weakness (M)

Commercial Property; The Office Market Is Feeling the Economic Jitters
Real estate executives differ on how severely Manhattan's office market will be affected by of current economic and stock market contractions; photos (M)

Interpublic to Buy True North for $2.1 Billion in Stock
There's a new No. 1 on Madison Avenue — again.

THE MEDIA BUSINESS: ADVERTISING; Interpublic Agrees to Buy True North
Interpublic Group of Companies acquires True North Communications in deal valued at $2.1 billion; deal lifts Interpublic to estimated $6.7 billion in revenue from $5.2 billion and estimated $62.5 billion in billings from $48.2 billion (M)

THE MARKETS: BONDS; Treasury Issues Dip on Profit Taking as Rate Move Is Awaited
Treasury bond prices fall; 10-year note falls 13/32, to 101 15/32; yield rises to 4.81 percent from 4.75 percent; price of 30-year bond falls 11/32, to 101 9/32; yield rises to 5.29 percent from 5.27 percent; graph on 3-month Treasury bills; chart on US key interest rates (M)

GDP Growth Falls to 4.6% as Unemployment Tops 1 Million People : A Sharp Slowdown in South Korea
South Korea's volatile economy slumped sharply in the last quarter of 2000, the Bank of Korea said Tuesday, underlining the difficulties that have dragged down business and industry here for the past year. .Perhaps most indicative of the country's conti

FED LOWERS RATES BY 0.5% AND HINTS OF FURTHER CUTS
Federal Reserve cuts federal funds rate to 5 percent from 5.5 percent and discount rate to 4.5 percent from 5 percent in continuing effort to spur economy; cites risk that economy could remain weak for extended period; indicates further rate cuts are possible before its May 15 meeting; investors, who hoped for bigger cuts, respond by sending stock prices lower; most big banks cut their prime lending rates to 8 percent from 8.5 percent; graphs of impact of cuts on consumers and of interest rates; photo (M)

Markets Deepen Economic Gloom
European stock markets plunged Thursday and the euro continued to tumble as the European Central Bank said it would reduce its growth forecast, fueling pessimism about the economic outlook, and more blue-chip companies issued profit warnings..The news

THE MEDIA BUSINESS: ADVERTISING; And the Oscar for the network willing to hold out the longest to sell costly spots goes to . . . ABC.
ABC completes sale of commercial time during 73rd annual Academy Awards scant three days before showtime, evidence of effect of struggling economy on Madison Avenue; spots will sell for just less than $1.4 million for each 30 seconds, compared with $1.2 million in 2000; photo (M)

Consumer Confidence Revives, Puzzling Some Economists
In an online promotion for its monthly report on consumer confidence, the Conference Board, a New York research group, poses a seemingly rhetorical question: "Who is it who drives the economy?"

Sudden Reversal in Outlook Surprises but Pleases Markets : Consumers in U.S. Regain Confidence
American consumers shook off their winter malaise this month, a private report indicated on Tuesday, lending support to a stock market that had been gripped with fears of an impending recession.. The Conference Board, a business research group, said its

Tremors in the Economy, Uncertainty in the Capital
News analysis: even before it becomes clear whether nation is slipping into recession or just enduring slowdown, Republicans and Democrats alike are scrambling to adjust to abrupt change in outlook from last year, when economic cockiness suffused policy and political considerations; continued bad economic news would affect broad swath of issues; accommodation can already be seen as dialogue over tax cuts shifts focus from using budget surplus to kick-starting economy (M)

The State of LVMH
LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton group managing director Myron E Ullman letter on March 25 article profiling company; says article was not balanced and created distorted version of reality; says company is continuing to thrive

'Mr. Yen' Sees More Trouble in Tokyo's Economic Future
Despite recent government attempts to put a positive spin on Japan's economic outlook, the world's second-largest economy could face a painful "double-dip" recession, Eisuke Sakakibara, a senior economist, warned over the weekend..Known as &q

U.N. Report Says U.S. Slowdown Is Damaging Other Economies
Forecasters from more than 60 countries warn that American slowdown is damaging many other economies around world, particulaly Asian nations with high-tech industries that have just begun to recover from 1997 regional crash; economists, meeting at UN, predict world economic growth will slow this year to 2.4 percent, from 4 percent; (M)
Business; Private Traders See Gold in Venture Capital Ruins

HEALING A HOSPITAL: A special report.; Alternative Medicine for Ailing Institution
Special report, Healing A Hospital, describes how one community hospital tackled its severe financial problems and stayed open, at time when most of nation's hospitals are ailing and many are closing; Armstrong County Memorial Hospital in Kittanning, Pa, 186-bed facility, hired turnaround expert as interim chief; he cut all salaries, dismissed some workers, reduced employees' time of by one vacation week and one holiday, and started search for new chief executive; new chief executive quickly hired financial analyst to clean up hospital's books; contracts with doctors were reviewed and tightened and bills to Medicare and managed care companies were checked for accuracy and completeness; number of board members was halved and those remaining were given training course in governance and strategic planning; new board hired salaried doctors to operate primary care centers around county, and those doctors referred many patients to Armstrong; many of moves have been painful and difficult, but result is that hospital has run surpluses in three of last four years, after losing $2.2 million in 1995; photos; graphs (L)

WORLD BUSINESS BRIEFING: ASIA; JAPAN: ECONOMIC OUTLOOK WEAKENS
Bank of Japan describes economy as being in 'state of adjustment,' amid flagging exports and slowing production; assessment represents downgrade from bank's view a month earlier (S)

World Business Briefing
EUROPE TURKEY: PLAN ENCOURAGES MARKETS Tur- key's currency, stocks and bonds rose on expectations that a government economic program announced over the weekend would win financial support from international lenders. The lira gained 2.6 percent; the benchmark stock index rose 4.9 percent; and the yield on the three-month bill maturing Aug. 8 fell 7 percentage points, to 131 percent. On Saturday, the economy minister, Kemal Dervis, announced the country's plans for ending an economic crisis that has dragged on for about two months. Turkish officials expect to complete talks with the International Monetary Fund and other lenders this week.   (Bloomberg News)

Income Tax Cut Proposed in Revised Budget
New York City Mayor Rudolph Giuliani will present revised budget containing 3.5 percent cut in city's income tax surcharge, costing city $180 million in revenues in next fiscal year (M)

It Urges ECB to Take ActionTo Ward Off Global Slowdown : IMF Says Rate Cut Is Needed in Europe
The head of the International Monetary Fund on Tuesday urged the European Central Bank to cut interest rates, saying it was vital that Europe play an active role in warding off a global economic slowdown.. European economic data dampened hopes for a rate

Bank Must Be 'Part of Solution,' Economist Says : IMF Jabs ECB on Growth
Only hours after the European Central Bank again elected to leave interest rates unchanged, the chief economist of the International Monetary Fund offered a sharp new critique of that immobility, saying that it was time for the ECB to "become part o

IMF's Economist Takes ECB to Task for Inaction
Only hours after the European Central Bank again elected to leave interest rates unchanged, the chief economist of the International Monetary Fund offered a sharp new critique of that decision, saying it was time for the ECB to "become part of the s

Tech Brief:BUSINESSES LIFT PC SALES
Sales of personal computers in Europe, the Middle East and Africa rose 7.2 percent in the first quarter from a year earlier as strong demand from businesses offset declining sales by consumers, the market research firm Dataquest Inc. reported. . "Lar

Asian Economies on a Knife Edge
After a strong rebound from the financial crisis of 1997 and 1998, East Asia's economic outlook is clouded with uncertainty. .The weakness of the Japanese currency, the yen, which has fallen more than 10 percent this year against the U.S. dollar, has d

As Jobs Are Lost, Consumers Slow Increase in Borrowing
Federal Reserve reports consumer debt outstanding grew by slower-than-expected $6 billion, March, up nearly 5 percent on annual basis from Feb, compared with $13.4 billion rise in Feb; slowdown, reflecting curbed spending on big-ticket items, suggests economic downturn might deepen (M)

THE MARKETS: Market Place; The Fed Cut Rates, and Wall Street Yawned
Market Place column on lackluster performance of stocks and bonds in wake of Federal Reserve's decision to cut short-term interest rate by half percentage point, to 4 percent; photo (M)

Team Bush: Partisan but Nimble
Pres George W Bush's economic team has set in motion many of biggest items on his agenda in its four months on the job; conservatives like team's focus on smaller government, deregulation and individual responsibility; Bush's ideological opponents say team's approach is fiscally irresponsible, a payoff to its corporate patrons and an effort to repackage right-wing policies in centrist guise; even some of harshest critics are impressed by team's discipline and political touch and its nimbleness in turning events to its own favor; lead player for now appears to be Lawrence B Lindsey, director of National Economic Council; others on team are Treasury Sec Paul H O'Neill, Mitchell E Daniels Jr, director of Office of Management and Budget, Robert B Zoellick, trade representative, R Glenn Hubbard, chairman of Council of Economic Advisers, Josh Bolten, Deputy White House chief of staff, and John B Taylor, nominee for under secretary of Treasury for international affairs; photos (M)

Analyst Sees Slow Quarter For Australia
Salomon Smith Barney economist Paul Brennan says fallout from collapse in March of Australia's second-largest general insurer, HIH Insurance, is spreading across economy, threatening to retard economic growth in second quarter; warning comes one day after government announces formation of committee to conduct full-scale inquiry into collapse and gives detailed plans for $265 million taxpayer-financed fund to help policyholders; Prime Min John Howard calls debacle 'major corporate collapse,' and financial services minister, Joe Hockey, describes it as 'most complex corporate collapse in Australia'; Brennan says he is otherwise optimistic about Australia's economic outlook but that HIH collapse is so big and messy that impact will be widely felt; predicts that Australia will manage 0.5 percent growth in gross domestic product in second quarter, in part because of HIH collapse (M)
Europe's Downturn Is 'Right Now'

THE MARKETS; Stocks Mixed In Europe And Asia
Stocks in Europe and Asia are mixed in light trading; US and London markets are closed (S)

Trenton Projects Shortfall Totaling Almost $1 Billion
New Jersey Acting Gov Donald DiFrancesco administration acknowledges nearly $1 billion shortfall; at hearing in May, administration failed to provide updated tax revenue projections, blaming volatile economy (M)

DataBank; Troubles Take a Heavy Toll on Optimism
Stock prices fell in week ended June 15 amid spate of warnings about second-quarter earnings; graphs; tables (M)

THE MARKETS: CURRENCIES
Japanese yen falls after Bank of Japan becomes more pessimistic with its June economic outlook, signaling country may be lapsing into recession; in New York, dollar settles at 123.36 yen, up from 123.08 yen; graph; chart (S)

The Fed Turns Less Aggressive
Editorial notes that Federal Reserve's latest interest rate cut is quarter of percentage point rather than half-point it resorted to in previous cuts; says Fed's restraint seems prudent, given fact that it has not had much time to observe effects of its past decisions; says if current slowdown feels like recession, it is because of heights from which economy has fallen

Banks Tightening Lending, Survey Finds
Office of the Comptroller of the Currency survey shows banks significantly tightened lending standards in last year (S)

Budget Surplus Could Dwindle, Bush Aide Says
Lawrence B Lindsey, director of White House's National Economic Council, raises possibility that tax revenues could fall about $56 billion short of $2 trillion that was anticipated for current fiscal year, but says there should still be substantial budget surplus, in range of $200 billion, if excess revenues from Social Security and Medicare are included; Democrats say revenue shortfall will put government at risk of running a deficit if Social Security and Medicare funds are excluded from calculations, the standard that Democrats want to use (M)

Crackdown Does Little to Help Burma's Economy
Until the police came for her this spring, Ma Khine Aye was known as the woman who ruled Burma's currency, the kyat..Operating openly from a shop marked by a signboard in downtown Rangoon, Ma Khine Aye changed dollars to kyat for foreign and local busi

Commercial Property/Hotels; Offering Business Travelers Less-Fancy Quarters
Harry Gross explains Marriott Courtyard hotels he is opening in New York, using concept of limited-service hotel in downtown location, without extensive public facilities; photo (M)

Greenspan Says More Action May Be Needed on Economy
Alan Greenspan, the Federal Reserve's chairman, said today that the American economy remained weak and hinted that the central bank might cut interest rates further

Greenspan Warns of Serious Risk But Says Rebound May Be in Sight
Alan Greenspan, Federal Reserve chairman, says economy remains weak and susceptible to more bad news; suggests central bank might cut interest rates at least once more this year; balances upbeat view of economy's long-term prospects against decidedly mixed forecast for rest of this year and into 2002; says economy has held up well, given all problems it has faced; appears before House Financial Services Committee to present Fed's twice-yearly economic report card; stock prices fall as investors focus on gloomy aspects of his testimony; photo (M)

But Fed Chief Also Sees Signs of a U.S. Turnaround : Greenspan Hints at More Cuts
America's central bankers are not ready to abandon their program of cutting interest rates to combat a recession threat, Alan Greenspan, the Federal Reserve Board chairman, told Congress on Wednesday, but he said tentative signs of a turnaround may be em

Forecasting Index Registers Third Consecutive Increase
Conference Board reports index of leading economic indicators rose 0.3 percent in June after 0.4 percent increase in May; Commerce Department says trade deficit narrowed 11.4 percent in May, to 16-month low of $28.3 billion; Labor Department says first-time claims for unemployment insurance fell 35,000 last week, to 414,000; graph (M)

Greenspan Hints at More Cuts but Sees a Recovery
America's central bankers are not ready to abandon their program of cutting interest rates to combat a recession threat, Alan Greenspan, the Federal Reserve Board chairman, told Congress, but he said tentative signs of a turnaround may be emerging. . &qu

Transition in Indonesia:Cause for Relief, Not for Celebration
In the short run, Indonesia will benefit from the replacement of Abdurrahman Wahid by Megawati Sukarnoputri. The longer-run implications are more negative..The positives are the ending, for now at least, of the struggle for power between the Parliament

ECONOMIC VIEW; Slowdown's Other Shoe, Hanging Over The Hudson

Metro Business Briefing
NEW JERSEY'S DEBT GROWS New Jersey's mounting debt is threatening the state's long-term financial strength and its ability to pay for basic services, especially in an economic downturn, according to a report. A recession would raise serious doubts about New Jersey's ability to maintain the current levels of aid to municipalities and schools, provide property tax relief and make grants to communities and social, medical and other programs, according to the report, issued by the New Jersey Policy Perspective, a nonprofit and nonpartisan research group. The state's long-term debt climbed to $15.7 billion last year, from $4 billion in 1990, and the state expects to borrow at least $9 billion more over the next five years. By 2010, the state will have to pay at least $2.8 billion in debt repayments, compared with the current $1.35 billion. About 6.2 percent of the state's budget last year went to pay debt-service costs, according to James Poole, the state's finance director. But merely looking at the debt misses the accomplishments that have been achieved with it, he said.   (Reuters) HELP WITH MANAGED CARE New Jersey residents confused by managed care health plans or frustrated by problems with such plans have a new telephone help line and interactive Web site to give them information about their rights. The new service, the Managed Health Care Consumer Assistance Program, is run by two nonprofit groups, New Jersey Protection and Advocacy Inc., in Trenton, which helps people with disabilities, and the Community Health Law Project, in South Orange, a legal aid group that helps elderly and disabled people. The toll-free phone service, at (888) 838-3180, is open during normal business hours on weekdays. It allows callers to speak confidentially with someone knowledgeable about their issues. The Web address is www.managedcarehelpline.org. The site is also confidential.   (AP)

HARDWARE SANMINA ISSUES EARNINGS WARNING The San- mina Corporation, which builds electronic equipment for other companies, said yesterday that sales and earnings this quarter would be less than forecast as customers reduce spending. Sales will be $600 million to $620 million for the fourth quarter, ending Sept. 29, the company said. Excluding acquisition-related expenses and charges, Sanmina expects to have a profit of 1 to 2 cents a share. The company was expected to earn 9 cents on sales of $734 million, according to the average estimates of analysts polled by Thomson Financial/First Call. Sanmina, which makes mobile telephones, personal computers and telecommunications gear, is experiencing falling demand for its products as sales slow for its customers. The company has been buying the manufacturing operations of other companies to add more services and increase efficiency to combat dropping sales.   (Bloomberg News) DECREASE IN CHIP SALES Worldwide sales of semiconductors fell more than 37 percent in July, to $10.86 billion, said a prominent industry trade group yesterday.
